title: Ars Live recap: When are the big rockets NASA desperately needs going to be ready?
gist: In an Ars Live panel, space analysts argued Blue Origin's more powerful 9x4 New Glenn variant needed for Artemis lunar missions could slip into the 2030s after the May pad explosion, making an all-in bet on SpaceX Starship the more believable path to landing US astronauts on the Moon this decade.

insights.claim: Blue Origin's current human-mission architecture would require four launches of a new, more powerful New Glenn variant (9x4, with nine first-stage and four upper-stage engines) versus the 7x2 variant that exploded in late May.
insights.speaker: Eric Berger
insights.so_what: A four-launch-per-mission architecture on an undeveloped rocket variant is a major execution and cadence risk for NASA's lunar plans.

insights.claim: Blue Origin has set no target date for the 9x4 New Glenn debut, though some sources indicate late 2027 or early 2028, which analyst Caleb Henry expects to slip by at least a year and a half.
insights.speaker: Eric Berger
insights.so_what: Applying a 1.5x schedule multiplier to stated rocket dates is a useful heuristic for modeling contractor slip risk.
insights.quote: I think 1.5 is usually a good rule, so a year and a half at least.

insights.claim: Adding engines to create the 9x4 variant makes the vehicle more complicated, not less, and is intended to enable new missions rather than accelerate the timeline.
insights.speaker: Eric Berger
insights.so_what: Undercuts optimism that the more capable variant will arrive quickly; expect further complexity-driven delays.
insights.quote: I think adding engines makes it more complicated, not less.

insights.claim: Podcast host Anthony Colangelo said it would not surprise him if 9x4 New Glenn development slipped into the 2030s.
insights.speaker: Eric Berger
insights.so_what: A 2030s slip would remove Blue Origin as a viable near-term lunar launch provider, concentrating risk on alternatives.
insights.locator: Colangelo said it would not surprise him

themes.theme: The case for going all-in on SpaceX Starship

insights.claim: Panelists argued the only realistic near-term path is going all-in on SpaceX Starship, citing its proven ability to scale launch cadence, plans to fly from Florida, two Texas pads, and existing infrastructure scale.
insights.speaker: Eric Berger
insights.so_what: Reinforces SpaceX's positioning as the default lunar-launch provider if Blue Origin falters, concentrating NASA dependence further.
insights.quote: I don't really see a way out, other than you're just all in on Starship for the next four or five years.
insights.locator: I don't really see a way out

insights.claim: The panel framed a company that can already fly 160 times a year scaling a new vehicle to tens of flights per year as a more believable pathway than building new pads and facilities for a complex multi-vehicle architecture.
insights.speaker: Eric Berger
insights.so_what: Existing launch cadence, not paper architecture, is the metric to weigh when betting on lunar-mission feasibility.
insights.quote: more complex than a company that can already fly 160 times a year flying a new vehicle tens of times a year
insights.locator: That's very quickly getting to a point
